blocks:
Ayahs

قُلْ هُوَ ٱللَّهُ أَحَدٌ (١)
Say, ‘He is Allah, the One. (1)
ٱللَّهُ ٱلصَّمَدُ (٢)
Allah is the All-embracing. (2)
لَمْ يَلِدْ وَلَمْ يُولَدْ (٣)
He neither begat, nor was begotten, (3)
وَلَمْ يَكُن لَّهُۥ كُفُوًا أَحَدٌۢ (٤)
nor has He any equal.’ (4)

Content and Virtue of Surah "Al-Ikhlas"

This Surah, as its names suggest (Surah Al-Ikhlas and Surah Al-Tawhid), speaks of the Oneness of the Lord and His Uniqueness. In four short verses, it describes the Oneness of Allah so comprehensively that nothing more is needed.

Regarding the occasion of its revelation, it is narrated from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام: "The Jews asked the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله to describe his Lord for them. The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله remained silent for three days and gave no answer until this Surah was revealed and provided their answer."

In some narrations, it is mentioned that the questioner was "Abdullah bin Suriya," one of the famous leaders of the Jews. In another narration, it is stated that "Abdullah bin Salam" asked this question of the Noble Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله in Mecca, then embraced Islam but kept his faith concealed for a time.

Other narrations state that the polytheists of Mecca asked this question [^1]. Some narrations also mention that the questioners were a group of Christians from Najran.

There is no contradiction among these narrations, as it is possible that this question was raised by all of them. This itself is evidence of the extraordinary greatness of this Surah, which answers the questions of various individuals and nations.

Regarding the virtue of reciting this Surah, many narrations are found in famous Islamic sources indicating its extraordinary greatness, including:

In a hadith from the Noble Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, we read that He said: "Is any of you unable to recite one-third of the Quran in a single night?" One of the attendees asked: "O Messenger of Allah! Who has the strength for such a task?" The Prophet replied: "Recite Qul Huwa Allahu Ahad (Surah Al-Ikhlas)."

In another hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, we read: "When the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله performed the funeral prayer for 'Sa'd bin Mu'adh,' He said: 'Seventy thousand angels, among whom was Gabriel, performed the prayer over his body! I asked Gabriel: By what deed did he deserve your prayer?' Gabriel replied: 'Because of his recitation of Qul Huwa Allahu Ahad while sitting, standing, riding, walking, and coming and going.'" [^1]

In another hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, we read: "Whoever passes a day and night and performs the five daily prayers but does not recite Qul Huwa Allahu Ahad in them, it is said to him: 'O servant of Allah! You are not among the performers of prayer!'" [^2]

In another hadith from the Noble Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, it is stated: "Whoever believes in Allah and the Last Day should not neglect reciting Surah Qul Huwa Allahu Ahad after every prayer, for whoever recites it, Allah gathers for him the good of this world and the Hereafter, and forgives him, his parents, and his children." [^3]

[^1]: Al-Mizan, Vol. 20, p. 546. [^1]: Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 10, p. 561. [^2] and [^3]: Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 10, p. 561, and other books of Tafsir and Hadith.

From another narration, it is understood that reciting this Surah upon entering the house increases sustenance and wards off poverty [^1]. The virtue of this Surah is more than can be contained in this brief summary; what we have cited is only a portion of it.

As for how Surah Qul Huwa Allahu Ahad is equivalent to one-third of the Quran:

Some have said it is because the Quran consists of "Laws," "Beliefs," and "History," and this Surah expresses the "Beliefs" section in a condensed form.

Others have said: The Quran is three parts: "The Origin," "The Return (Resurrection)," and "What lies between the two," and this Surah explains the first part.

It is an acceptable statement that approximately one-third of the Quran discusses Monotheism (Tawhid), and its essence is contained in Surah Al-Tawhid.

We conclude this discussion with another hadith regarding the greatness of this Surah:

Imam Ali bin al-Husayn عليه السلام was asked about Surah Al-Tawhid. He replied:

"Indeed, Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, knew that in the end of times there would be nations who investigate deeply (into matters). Therefore, Allah the Exalted revealed Qul Huwa Allahu Ahad and the verses of Surah Al-Hadid up to His words: 'And He is Knowing of that within the breasts.' So whoever seeks beyond that has perished." [^2]

[^1]: Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 10, p. 561, and other books of Tafsir and Hadith. [^2]: Usul al-Kafi, Vol. 1, Chapter: Al-Nisbah, Hadith 3.

In the Name of Allah, the Entirely Merciful, the Especially Merciful

Say, "He is Allah, [who is] One, (1) Allah, the Eternal Refuge. (2) He neither begets nor is born, (3) Nor is there to Him any equivalent." (4)

Commentary

He is One and Peerless

The first verse of this Surah, in response to repeated questions from various nations or individuals regarding the attributes of the Lord, says: "Say, He is Allah, the One and Unique" (Qul Huwa Allahu Ahad) [^1]. The sentence begins with the pronoun "Huwa" (He), which is a third-person singular pronoun and refers to an ambiguous concept. In reality, it is a code and an allusion to the fact that His Sacred Essence is in the ultimate state of hiddenness and beyond the reach of limited human thoughts, even though His signs have filled the world such that He is more manifest and apparent than anything else. As we read in verse 53 of Surah Fussilat: "We will show them Our signs in the horizons and within themselves until it becomes clear to them that He is the Truth."

Then, it unveils this unknown reality and says: "He is Allah, the Unique and One."

Furthermore, the meaning of "Qul" (Say) here is to express and declare this reality.

In a hadith from Imam al-Baqir عليه السلام, it is mentioned that after stating this, he said: "The disbelievers and idolaters used to point to their idols with demonstrative pronouns, saying: 'These are our gods, O Muhammad! You too describe your God so that we may see and perceive Him.' Then Allah revealed these verses: Qul Huwa Allahu Ahad... The 'Ha' in 'Huwa' refers to confirmation and drawing attention to the matter, and the 'Waw' is the third-person pronoun referring to One who is absent from the sight of eyes and far from the touch of the senses." [^1]

In another hadith from the Commander of the Faithful, Ali عليه السلام, we read that he said: "On the night of the Battle of Badr, I saw 'Khidr' in a dream. I asked him to teach me something by which I might be victorious over the enemies. He said: Say: Ya Hu, ya man la Hu illa Hu (O He, O One whom there is no 'He' but He). When morning came, I related the event to the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله. He said: 'O Ali! You have been taught the Greatest Name (al-Ism al-A'zam).' Then this phrase was constantly on my tongue during the Battle of Badr..." [^2] When "Ammar Yasir" heard the Commander of the Faithful عليه السلام reciting this dhikr on the day of Siffin during the battle, he asked: "What are these allusions?" He replied: "The Greatest Name of Allah and the pillar of Monotheism!" [^3]

[^1]: Some have taken "Huwa" here as the "pronoun of the matter" (damir al-sha'n), thus the meaning would be: "The matter is this: Allah is One." However, it is better that "Huwa" refers to the Pure Essence of Allah which was unknown and ambiguous to the questioners. Thus, "Huwa" would be the subject (mubtada), "Allah" the predicate (khabar), and "Ahad" a second predicate. [^1]: Bihar al-Anwar, Vol. 3, p. 221, Hadith 12 (summarized). [^2] and [^3]: Bihar al-Anwar, Vol. 3, p. 222.

"Allah" is the proper name for God, and the meaning of the Imam's عليه السلام words is that in this single word, all His attributes of Majesty (Jalal) and Beauty (Jamal) are alluded to. For this reason, it is called the Greatest Name of Allah. This name is applied to none but God, whereas other names of Allah usually refer to one of His attributes of Beauty or Majesty, such as the All-Knowing (Alim), the Creator (Khaliq), and the Provider (Raziq), and are often applied to others as well (such as merciful, generous, knowledgeable, powerful, etc.). Nevertheless, its root has a descriptive meaning, originally derived from "Walah" meaning "bewilderment," because intellects are bewildered in His Pure Essence. As stated in a hadith from the Commander of the Faithful, Ali عليه السلام:

"Allah means the One who is worshipped, in whom the creation is bewildered, and towards whom they yearn. Allah is He who is hidden from the perception of sights, veiled from imaginations and passing thoughts." [^1]

Sometimes it is also considered to be from the root "Alahah" (on the pattern of and meaning worship), and originally it is "Al-Ilah," meaning "the only true Deity."

But as we said, whatever its root may be, it later became a "proper name" and refers to that Essence which encompasses all perfect attributes and is free from any defect or deficiency.

This holy name is repeated nearly "one thousand times" in the Holy Quran, and no other name among His holy names appears this much in the Quran. It is a name that illuminates the heart, grants strength and peace to man, and immerses him in a world of light and purity.

As for the word "Ahad," it is from the same root as "Wahdah" (unity). Therefore, some have interpreted "Ahad" and "Wahid" to have the same meaning, believing both refer to that Essence which is unique and solitary in every respect—Unique in knowledge, peerless in power, one in mercy and compassion, and in short, incomparable in every way.

However, some believe there is a difference between "Ahad" and "Wahid." "Ahad" is said of an Essence that does not accept plurality, neither externally nor in the mind; therefore, it cannot be counted and never enters into numbering. This is unlike "Wahid," for which a second and third can be imagined, either externally or in the mind. Thus, we sometimes say "Not one (ahad) of that crowd came," meaning no one at all came. But when we say "One (wahid) did not come," it is possible that two or more people did come. [^1] However, this distinction does not align well with its usage in the Holy Quran and hadiths.

Some also believe: "Ahad" refers to the simplicity of Allah's Essence as opposed to external or intellectual composite parts (genus and species, essence and existence), while "Wahid" refers to the uniqueness of His Essence as opposed to external pluralities.

In a hadith from Imam Muhammad al-Baqir عليه السلام, we read: "'Ahad' is the unique individual, and 'Ahad' and 'Wahid' have one meaning; it is the solitary Essence for whom there is no peer or likeness. 'Tawhid' is the acknowledgment of His uniqueness, unity, and solitude."

In the continuation of this same hadith, we read: "'Wahid' is not from numbers, rather 'Wahid' is the foundation of numbers; numbers begin from two. Therefore, the meaning of 'Allah Ahad' is the Deity whose Essence humans are incapable of perceiving and whose quality they are unable to encompass. He is solitary in His Divinity and transcendent above the attributes of creatures." [^2] In the Holy Quran, both "Wahid" and "Ahad" are applied to the Pure Essence of Allah.

Interestingly, it is mentioned in Tawhid al-Saduq that a Bedouin man stood up on the day of the Battle of "Jamal" and said: "O Commander of the Faithful! Do you say that Allah is One? What does 'One' mean?"

Suddenly, people attacked him from all sides, saying: "O Bedouin! What kind of question is this? Do you not see how much the mind of the Commander of the Faithful is occupied with the matter of war? Every word has its place and every point its occasion!" The Commander of the Faithful عليه السلام said: "Leave him be, for what he desires is exactly what we desire from this enemy group" (He asks about Monotheism, and we also invite our opponents to the unity of the word).

Then he said: "O Bedouin! Our saying that God is 'One' can have four meanings, two of which are not correct regarding God, and two of which are correct.

As for the two that are not correct: Numerical unity; this is not permissible for God (to say He is one and not two, for the concept of this statement is that a second is imaginable for Him but does not exist, whereas certainly no second is imaginable for the Infinite Essence of the Truth), because something that has no second does not enter the category of numbers. Do you not see that Allah declared those who said: 'Indeed, Allah is the third of three' to be disbelievers?

Another meaning of 'One' that is not permissible for God is to mean 'generic unity' (unity of kind), such as when we say so-and-so is one of the people. This too is not permissible for God (because God has no kind or genus). The concept of this statement is resemblance, and God is higher and greater than any likeness or peer.

As for the two concepts that are true regarding God: First, it is said Allah is One meaning there is no likeness for Him among the things of the world; yes, our Lord is such.

Second, it is said our Lord is 'Ahad al-Ma'na' (One in Meaning), meaning His Essence is not divisible, neither externally, nor in the intellect, nor in the imagination; yes, the Great God is such." [^1]

In short, Allah is Ahad and Wahid, and He is Unique and One, not in the sense of numerical, generic, or specific unity, but in the sense of Essential Unity (al-Wahdah al-Dhatiyyah). In clearer terms, His Uniqueness means the non-existence of any like, equal, resemblance, or peer for Him.

The proof for this is also clear: He is an Essence that is infinite in every respect, and it is certain that two essences infinite in every respect are unimaginable. Because if there were two essences, both would become limited; this one would lack the perfections of that one, and that one would lack the perfections of this one (reflect carefully).

In the next verse, in another description of that Unique Sacred Essence, it says: "He is the God whom all the needy intend (turn to)" (Allahu al-Samad).

Many meanings have been mentioned for "Al-Samad" in narrations and the words of commentators and linguists:

"Raghib" says in Al-Mufradat: "Samad" means a master and great one to whom people turn for the fulfillment of affairs. Some have said: "Samad" means something that is not hollow but is solid/full.

In Maqayis al-Lughah, it is stated that "Samad" has two main roots: one meaning "intent" (qasd) and the other meaning "solidity and firmness." The reason Allah the Exalted is called "Samad" is that His servants intend His threshold.

Perhaps for this reason, the following multiple meanings are also mentioned in lexicons for "Samad": A great person who is at the peak of greatness; one to whom people turn for their needs; one above whom there is nothing; one who is eternal and remains after the annihilation of creation.

Therefore, Imam Husayn bin Ali عليه السلام, in a hadith, explained five meanings for "Samad": 1. "Samad" is the one who is at the peak of sovereignty and mastery. 2. "Samad" is the Eternal, Pre-eternal, and Everlasting Essence. 3. "Samad" is an existence that has no interior (is not hollow). 4. "Samad" is the one who does not eat or drink. 5. "Samad" is the one who does not sleep. [^1]

In another expression, it is said: "Samad is the one who is Self-Subsistent and independent of others." "Samad" is the one who has no changes, becoming, or corruption.

It is narrated from Imam Ali bin al-Husayn عليه السلام that he said: "'Samad' is the one who has no partner, for whom the preservation of anything is not difficult, and from whom nothing remains hidden." [^2] Some have also said: "'Samad' is the one who, whenever He intends something, says 'Be,' and it immediately becomes."

In a hadith, it is mentioned that the people of "Basra" wrote a letter to Imam Husayn عليه السلام asking about the meaning of "Samad." The Imam عليه السلام replied to them:

"In the Name of Allah, the Entirely Merciful, the Especially Merciful. As for what follows: Do not engage in dispute or conversation regarding the Quran without knowledge, for I heard my grandfather the Messenger of Allah saying: 'Whoever speaks without knowledge must take his place in the fire prepared for him.' Allah Himself has interpreted 'Samad': Lam yalid wa lam yulad wa lam yakun lahu kufuwan ahad: 'He neither begot, nor was He begotten, and there is none like Him.' ... Yes, Allah the 'Samad' is the one who did not come into being from anything, is not contained in anything, and is not placed upon anything. He is the Originator of things and their Creator. He brought everything into existence by His power. What He created for annihilation disintegrates by His will, and what He created for permanence remains by His knowledge. This is Allah, the Samad..." [^1]

Finally, in another hadith, we read that "Muhammad bin Hanafiyyah" asked "the Commander of the Faithful" Ali عليه السلام about "Samad." The Imam عليه السلام said:

"The interpretation of Samad is that He is neither a name nor a body; He has no like and no resemblance; He has no form and no image; no limit and no boundaries; no location and no place; no 'how' and no 'where'; neither here nor there; He is neither full nor empty; He is neither standing nor sitting; He has neither stillness nor motion; He is neither dark nor luminous; He is neither spiritual nor psychic. Yet, no place is empty of Him, and no place can contain Him. He has no color, nor has He occurred to a human heart, nor does He have a scent. All of these are negated from His Pure Essence." [^2]

This hadith clearly shows that "Samad" has a very comprehensive and vast meaning that negates all attributes of creatures from His Sacred Presence. This is because specific and limited names, as well as physicality, color, scent, place, stillness, motion, quality, limits, and the like, are all attributes of possible beings and creatures—rather, they are mostly attributes of the material world—and we know that Allah is greater and higher than all of these.

In recent discoveries, it is stated that all things in the material world are composed of very small particles called "atoms." The "atom" itself is composed of two main parts: a central nucleus and electrons orbiting around it. Surprisingly, there is a vast distance between that nucleus and the electrons (vast in comparison to the volume of the atom), such that if this distance were removed, objects would become so small that it would be astonishing to us.

For example, if the atomic distances of the particles of a human being were removed and he were completely compressed, he might become a particle difficult to see with the eye, yet he would still possess the entire weight of a human body (for instance, this tiny particle would weigh 60 kg).

Some, using this scientific discovery and considering that one of the meanings of "Samad" is an existence that is not hollow or empty, have concluded that the Quran intends to negate any physicality from God with this expression. This is because all bodies are composed of atoms, and atoms are hollow. In this way, the verse could be one of the scientific miracles of the Quran.

However, it must not be forgotten that "Samad" in its original linguistic sense means a great person to whom all the needy turn, and who is full and complete in every respect. Apparently, the rest of the meanings and interpretations mentioned for it return to this same root.

Then, in the next verse, it proceeds to refute the beliefs of the "Christians," "Jews," and "Arab polytheists" who attributed a child or a father to Allah, saying: "He neither begets nor is born" (Lam yalid wa lam yulad).

Opposing this statement is the talk of those who believed in the Trinity (three gods): the Father God, the Son God, and the Holy Spirit! The Christians considered "Messiah" to be the son of God, and the Jews considered "Ezra" (Uzayr) to be His son: "The Jews said, 'Ezra is the son of Allah'; and the Christians said, 'The Messiah is the son of Allah.' That is their statement from their mouths; they imitate the saying of those who disbelieved before [them]. May Allah destroy them; how are they deluded?" (At-Tawbah: 30).

The Arab polytheists also believed that the angels were the daughters of God! "And they falsely attributed to Him sons and daughters without knowledge" (Al-An'am: 100).

From some narrations, it is understood that "birth" in the verse Lam yalid wa lam yulad has a broader meaning, negating any emergence of material or subtle things from Him, or the emergence of that Sacred Essence from other material or subtle things.

As in the same letter that Imam Husayn عليه السلام wrote in response to the people of "Basra" regarding the interpretation of Samad, the phrase Lam yalid wa lam yulad was interpreted as follows: "Lam yalid means nothing emerged from Him—neither material things like a child and other things that emerge from creatures (like milk from a mother's breast), nor subtle things like the soul, nor various accidents like sleep, imagination, grief, sadness, happiness, laughter, weeping, fear, hope, longing, boredom, hunger, and satiety. Allah is transcendent above anything emerging from Him.

And He is also transcendent above being born from a material or subtle thing... like the emergence of a living being from another being, plants from the earth, water from a spring, fruit from trees, and the emergence of subtle things from their sources, like seeing from the eye, hearing from the ear, smelling from the nose, tasting from the mouth, speech from the tongue, knowledge and discernment from the heart, and a spark of fire from a stone." [^1]

According to this hadith, "birth" has an expansive meaning that includes any emergence or derivation of one thing from another. This is, in fact, the second meaning of the verse, while the first and apparent meaning was what was stated at the beginning. Furthermore, the second meaning is fully understandable through an analysis of the first meaning, for if Allah has no child, it is because He is free from the accidents of matter. This same logic applies to other accidents of matter (reflect carefully).

Finally, in the last verse of this Surah, it brings the matter regarding the attributes of God to the stage of perfection, saying: "Nor is there to Him any equivalent" (Wa lam yakun lahu kufuwan ahad) [^2]. "Kufu'" originally means an equal in rank, status, and power, and was subsequently applied to any kind of like or peer.

According to this verse, all accidents of creatures, attributes of beings, and any kind of deficiency or limitation are negated from His Pure Essence. This is the same Essential and Attributive Monotheism (al-Tawhid al-Dhatiyyah wa al-Sifatiyyah), as opposed to numerical and generic monotheism mentioned at the beginning of the commentary of this Surah.

Therefore, He has no peer in His Essence, no like in His attributes, and no equal in His actions; He is unique and incomparable in every respect.

The Commander of the Faithful, Ali عليه السلام, says in one of the sermons of Nahj al-Balaghah: "He did not beget, lest He be born... and He was not born, lest He be limited... and He has no equal to be His peer, and no likeness is imagined for Him to be His equal." [^1] This is a beautiful interpretation that expresses the most sublime subtleties of Monotheism (Peace be upon you, O Commander of the Faithful).

[^1]: Bihar al-Anwar, Vol. 3, p. 224. [^2]: "Ahad" is the subject of "kana" and "kufuwan" is its predicate. [^1]: Nahj al-Balaghah, Sermon 186.

Key Points

1- Proofs of Monotheism

Monotheism (Tawhid), meaning the uniqueness of Allah's Essence and the non-existence of any peer or likeness for Him, can be proven through numerous intellectual arguments in addition to traditional proofs and Quranic verses. We present a portion of them here in a condensed form:

1- The Argument of Pure Existence (Sarf al-Wujud)

The summary is that Allah is Absolute Existence, and there is no condition, constraint, or limit for Him. Such an existence will certainly be infinite, because if it were to have a limitation, it would have to be tainted with non-existence ('adam). The Sacred Essence from which existence gushes forth will never necessitate non-existence, and there is nothing external to impose non-existence upon Him. Therefore, He is not limited by any boundary.

On the other hand, two infinite existences are unimaginable in the world. For if two beings existed, each would necessarily lack the perfections of the other—meaning it would not possess its perfections—and thus both would be limited. This itself is a clear proof of the uniqueness of the Essence of the Necessary Being (reflect carefully).

2- The Scientific Argument

When we look at this vast universe, we initially see the world as scattered beings: earth, sky, sun, moon, stars, and various plants and animals. However, the more we reflect, the more we see that the parts and particles of this world are so related and connected that they collectively form a single cohesive unit, and a series of specific laws govern this entire world.

The more human science and knowledge progress, the more the unity and cohesion of the parts of this world become apparent, to the point that sometimes an experiment on a small sample (like an apple falling from a tree) leads to the discovery of a great law that governs the entire universe (as happened with "Newton" and the "Law of Gravity").

This unity of the system of existence, the laws governing it, and the cohesion and integration among its parts show that its Creator is One and Unique.

3- The Argument of Mutual Hindrance (Tamānu') — (A Scientific-Philosophical Proof)

Another proof mentioned for proving the uniqueness of Allah's Essence, which the Quran inspires in verse 22 of Surah Al-Anbiya, is the Argument of Mutual Hindrance. It says: "Had there been within the heavens and earth gods besides Allah, they both would have been ruined. So exalted is Allah, Lord of the Throne, above what they describe!" We have explained this proof in detail in Volume 13, page 381, under the title "The Argument of Mutual Hindrance."

4- The Universal Call of the Prophets to the One God

This is another proof for proving Monotheism. For if there were two Necessary Beings in the world, both would have to be sources of grace (fayd), because it is impossible for an infinitely perfect being to be stingy in radiating light; the absence of grace is a defect for a perfect being, and His being Wise requires Him to include everyone in His grace.

This grace has two branches: Ontological Grace (in the world of creation) and Legislative Grace (in the world of guidance). Therefore, if multiple gods existed, messengers would have to come from all of them to deliver their legislative grace to everyone.

Imam Ali عليه السلام says in his testament to his noble son, Imam al-Mujtaba عليه السلام:

"Know, my son, that if your Lord had a partner, his messengers would have come to you, and you would have seen the traces of his kingdom and power, and you would have known his actions and attributes. But He is One God as He has described Himself." [^1]

[^1]: Nahj al-Balaghah, Testament to Imam al-Mujtaba (Letters section: Letter 31).

These are all proofs of the uniqueness of His Essence. As for the proof of the absence of any composition or parts in His Pure Essence, it is clear: for if He had external parts, He would naturally be dependent on them, and dependency is irrational for the Necessary Being.

And if "intellectual parts" (composition of essence and existence, or genus and species) are meant, that too is impossible. For composition of "essence" and "existence" is a branch of being limited, while we know His existence is infinite. And composition of "genus" and "species" is a branch of having an essence; something that has no essence has no genus or species.

2- The Fruitful Branches of Monotheism

Usually, four branches are mentioned for Monotheism:

1- Monotheism of Essence (Tawhid al-Dhat) (As explained above).

2- Monotheism of Attributes (Tawhid al-Sifat) Meaning His attributes are not separate from His Essence, nor are they separate from one another. For example, our "knowledge" and "power" are two attributes accidental to our essence; our essence is one thing and our knowledge and power are another. Just as "knowledge" and "power" are separate in us—the center of knowledge is our soul, and the center of physical power is our arms and muscles. But in Allah, neither are His attributes additional to His Essence, nor are they separate from one another; rather, He is an existence that is entirely knowledge, entirely power, entirely pre-eternity and eternity.

If it were otherwise, it would necessitate composition, and if He were composite, He would be in need of parts, and a needy thing will never be the Necessary Being.

3- Monotheism of Actions (Tawhid al-Af'ali) Meaning every existence, every movement, and every action in the world returns to the Pure Essence of God. He is the Cause of causes and the Ultimate Cause. Even the actions that proceed from us are, in one sense, from Him; He has given us power, choice, and freedom of will. Therefore, while we are the actors of our own deeds and are responsible for them, from one perspective, the Actor is Allah, because everything we have returns to Him (There is no effect in existence except Allah).

4- Monotheism in Worship (Tawhid fi al-'Ibadah) Meaning He alone must be worshipped, and none besides Him is worthy of servitude. For worship must be for one who is Absolute Perfection and the Perfection of the Absolute; one who is independent of all, the Giver of all blessings, and the Creator of all beings. These attributes do not gather except in His Pure Essence.

The main goal of worship is to find a way to the proximity of that Absolute Perfection and Infinite Existence, and to reflect a ray of His attributes of perfection and beauty within the soul, the result of which is distancing oneself from whims and desires and turning toward self-building and purification of the soul.

This goal is not possible except through the worship of "Allah," who is that Absolute Perfection.

3- Branches of Monotheism of Actions

"Monotheism of Actions" in turn has many branches. Here we point to six of its most important sub-branches:

1- Monotheism of Creatorship (Tawhid al-Khaliqiyyah) As the Quran says: "Say, 'Allah is the Creator of all things'" (Ar-Ra'd: 16). The reason is also clear: when it was proven by previous arguments that the Necessary Being is One, and everything other than Him is a possible being, therefore the Creator of all beings will also be One.

2- Monotheism of Lordship (Tawhid al-Rububiyyah) Meaning the Disposer, Manager, Sustainer, and Regulator of the universe is only Allah. As the Quran says: "Say, 'Is it other than Allah I should desire as a lord while He is the Lord of all things?'" (Al-An'am: 164). The reason for this is also the unity of the Necessary Being and the Monotheism of the Creator in the universe.

3- Monotheism in Legislation (Tawhid fi al-Tashri') As the Quran says: "And whoever does not judge by what Allah has revealed—then it is those who are the disbelievers" (Al-Ma'idah: 44). Because when we have proven that He is the Manager and Disposer, certainly none other than Him will have the competence for legislation, as none other than Him has a share in the management of the world to enact laws harmonious with the system of creation.

4- Monotheism in Ownership (Tawhid fi al-Malikiyyah) Whether it be "real ownership," meaning ontological authority over something, or "legal ownership," meaning lawful authority over something—all of these are from Him. As the Quran says: "And to Allah belongs the dominion of the heavens and the earth" (Al-Imran: 189). And He also says: "And spend out of that in which He has made you successive inheritors" (Al-Hadid: 7). The reason for this is the same Monotheism in Creatorship; when He is the Creator of all things, naturally He is also the Owner of all things. Therefore, every ownership must stem from His ownership.

5- Monotheism of Sovereignty (Tawhid al-Hakimiyyah) Certainly, human society needs government, because collective life is not possible without government. The division of responsibilities, the organization of programs, the implementation of management, and the prevention of transgressions and violations are only possible through government.

On the other hand, the principle of human freedom says no one has the right of sovereignty over anyone else, unless the Original Owner and True Master grants permission. It is from here that we reject any government that does not lead back to Divine government. And it is also from here that we consider the legitimacy of government to belong to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, then the Infallible Imams عليه السلام, and after them to the jurist who meets all the necessary conditions (faqih jami' al-shara'it).

Of course, it is possible for people to give permission to someone to rule over them, but since the agreement of every single individual in society is usually impossible, such a government is practically impossible [^1]. Of course, it should not be forgotten that Monotheism of Lordship relates to the world of creation, and Monotheism of Legislation and Sovereignty relates to the world of law.

The Holy Quran says: "Legislation is not but for Allah" (Al-An'am: 57).

6- Monotheism of Obedience (Tawhid al-Ita'ah) Meaning the only "One whose obedience is mandatory" in the world is the Pure Essence of God. The legitimacy of obeying any other authority must stem from here—meaning obedience to them is considered obedience to God.

The reason is also clear: when sovereignty belongs exclusively to Him, being the one obeyed also belongs exclusively to Him. Therefore, we consider obedience to the Prophets عليه السلام, the Infallible Imams, and their successors as a reflection of obedience to God. The Quran says: "O you who have believed, obey Allah and obey the Messenger and those in authority among you (the Infallible Imams)" (An-Nisa: 59).

And He also says: "He who obeys the Messenger has obeyed Allah" (An-Nisa: 80).

Of course, each of the above discussions deserves extensive explanation, but we have stated them concisely so as not to deviate from the form of a commentary discussion.

O Allah! Keep us firm on the path of Monotheism throughout our lives. O Lord! The branches of polytheism (shirk), like the branches of Monotheism, are many, and salvation from polytheism is not possible except by Your grace; include us in Your favors. O God! Make us live with Monotheism, die with Monotheism, and be resurrected with the reality of Monotheism.

Amen, O Lord of the Worlds. End of Surah Al-Ikhlas.

[^1]: Therefore, if a government is determined through public votes and the majority, it must be executed through the faqih jami' al-shara'it so that it gains Divine legitimacy.
